title: Skills and Connections: AI That Plugs Into Everything
subtitle: Tools, connectors, and using your own information
theme: Today we turn a clever talker into a capable doer — by showing how AI plugs into the apps, documents, and tools you already use every day.
learningObjectives: Explain why a smart AI becomes far more useful once it can connect to the apps and data you already use
Describe an API in plain words as a 'plug' that lets two systems talk to each other
Recall how RAG lets an AI read and answer from your own documents
Understand connectors and MCP as a universal adapter that saves everyone from building one-off plugs
Trace how tools, connectors, and your data combine into the real AI products people pay for in 2026
storyHook: Imagine a brilliant new assistant who has read nearly everything and gives wonderful advice — but sits in a locked room with no phone, no email, and no filing cabinet. Smart, yes. Useful at your actual job? Not yet. Today is the day we hand that assistant a phone, a set of keys, and access to your files — and watch a clever talker turn into a real helper.

slides.title: Welcome: Giving AI Hands, Not Just a Brain
slides.body:
For most of this course we built up an AI that can *understand* and *talk*. It reads your words, thinks them over, and replies. That is amazing — but on its own, it is a bit like a genius locked in a quiet room with no door.

Think about your own day. You don't just *think*; you check your calendar, send an email, look something up, open a document. Your usefulness comes partly from being *connected* to things.

Today is about exactly that: how AI gets connected. We'll see how it can reach into the apps you already use, how systems 'plug' into each other, how AI can read *your* documents (not just the internet), and how a tidy new standard makes all of this easy.

By the end, you'll understand how a chatbot quietly becomes a product that books your travel, drafts replies in your inbox, or answers questions from your company's own files. The leap from 'clever' to 'genuinely helpful' happens right here.
slides.speakerNotes: Set the emotional frame: today is the 'aha' day where AI stops being a toy and becomes a tool. Keep it light and exciting.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, almost every AI product you hear about — from email helpers to customer-support bots — is really 'a smart model plus connections,' which is exactly what today explains.

slides.title: Connecting AI to the Apps You Already Use
slides.body:
On its own, an AI model is like a knowledgeable friend on a phone call: it can advise you, but it can't reach through the phone to actually *do* the thing. Connecting it to your apps is like giving that friend a desk in your office with access to your tools.

What does 'connecting' look like in practice? You let the AI peek at your calendar so it can suggest meeting times. You let it draft a message inside your email. You let it pull a number from your spreadsheet. The AI doesn't magically gain these powers — someone wires it up to each app, one careful connection at a time.

The key shift is from *answering* to *acting*. Before, you'd ask, 'What should my reply say?' and copy-paste it yourself. Connected, you can say, 'Reply and let them know I'm free Thursday,' and it happens.

Importantly, these connections come with permission. Just like you'd decide which rooms a new assistant can enter, you decide which apps the AI may touch — and good products always ask first before doing something big.
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize permission and trust early — beginners worry about an AI 'taking over.' Frame connections as opt-in.
slides.marketConnection: Today's AI assistants in tools like email, Slack, and Notion work because they're explicitly connected to those apps with your permission.

slides.title: APIs: The Universal Plugs Between Systems
slides.body:
So how does one piece of software 'talk' to another? Through something called an **API** — short for Application Programming Interface. That sounds scary, so let's translate it: an API is simply a **plug and socket** that lets two systems connect in an agreed-upon way.

Think of the wall sockets in your home. You don't need to know how the power station works; you just plug in, and it works — because everyone agreed on the shape of the plug. An API is that agreement for software. The weather app on your phone doesn't *contain* the weather; it plugs into a weather service's API and asks, 'What's it like in Mumbai?' The service answers, and your app shows it.

For AI, APIs are the doorways to the world. Want the AI to check a flight price? There's an API for that. Send a message? Another API. The AI sends a polite, structured request — 'please do this' — and gets back a tidy answer.

The beauty: nobody rebuilds the airline or the email system. They just expose a plug, and others connect to it.
slides.speakerNotes: The plug-and-socket analogy is the anchor — repeat it. Avoid any code; keep it physical and intuitive.
slides.marketConnection: Nearly every app on your phone quietly uses dozens of APIs in 2026; AI assistants ride on these same plugs to get things done.

slides.title: A Story: The Assistant Who Got the Keys
slides.body:
Picture a small travel agency that hires a sharp new assistant named Mira. On day one, Mira knows everything about destinations, visas, and good hotels — but she has no logins. A customer asks her to book a trip, and all she can do is *describe* how she'd do it. Frustrating for everyone.

On day two, the manager gives Mira three things: the keys to the booking system, access to the company email, and the customer file cabinet. Suddenly Mira is unstoppable. A request comes in — 'Family of four, Goa, next month, budget-friendly' — and Mira checks the booking API for flights, pulls the family's preferences from their file, drafts a confirmation email, and waits for a thumbs-up before sending.

Nothing about Mira's *intelligence* changed between day one and day two. What changed was her *connections*. The keys (APIs), the file cabinet (your documents), and a clear rule to confirm before acting turned a smart advisor into a productive teammate.

That is the whole story of modern AI products in miniature: same brain, brand-new reach.
slides.speakerNotes: Use Mira to make connections emotionally concrete. Stress that intelligence didn't change — access did.
slides.marketConnection: Real 2026 AI agents work just like Mira: a capable model given keys to booking, email, and customer data, with a confirm-before-acting safety step.

slides.title: Using Your Own Documents (RAG, Gently Revisited)
slides.body:
Remember Day 17, when we gave AI a memory? There's a cousin idea that's just as important: letting AI answer from **your own documents**. The technique is called **RAG** — Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Let's unpack that name kindly.

Imagine an open-book exam. Instead of memorizing everything, you bring a folder of notes. When a question comes, you *flip to the right page*, read it, then answer in your own words. That's RAG. 'Retrieval' is flipping to the right page; 'generation' is writing the answer.

Why does this matter? The base AI learned from the public internet up to a certain date. It doesn't know your company handbook, your contracts, or last week's meeting notes. RAG lets it search *your* files first, grab the relevant bits, and answer using them — with far fewer made-up details, because it's reading rather than guessing.

So a support bot can answer using your actual product manual. A lawyer's assistant can cite the real contract. The AI stays general and smart, but now it speaks with *your* facts.
slides.speakerNotes: This is a recap of an earlier idea, so re-explain warmly and keep the open-book-exam analogy front and center.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, most company chatbots and 'ask your docs' tools run on RAG so they answer from internal files instead of the open web.

slides.title: Connectors and MCP: The Universal Adapter
slides.body:
Here's a quiet problem. If every AI has to be wired by hand to every app — email, calendar, files, your company's tools — that's thousands of one-off plugs. Exhausting, and everyone reinvents the same wiring.

The fix is a **connector**, and a popular shared standard for connectors called **MCP** (Model Context Protocol). Don't worry about the long name — think of MCP as a **universal travel adapter**. You know how a single adapter lets your charger work in any country's socket? MCP is that idea for AI: one agreed-upon shape so any AI can plug into any tool that 'speaks MCP.'

Before, connecting an AI to a new app meant custom work each time. With MCP, the app provides one standard connector, and *any* MCP-aware AI can use it instantly. Build the plug once; everyone benefits.

This is why, in 2026, you can often add a new capability to your AI assistant — your notes app, your project tracker — almost like installing an app on a phone. The universal adapter did the hard part, so you don't have to.
slides.speakerNotes: Universal travel adapter is the killer analogy. The point of MCP is 'build once, plug in anywhere' — don't overcomplicate.
slides.marketConnection: MCP took off across the industry in 2025-2026, so many AI tools now offer one-click connectors to apps like Slack, Google Drive, and GitHub.

slides.title: How These Building Blocks Become Real Products
slides.body:
Now let's snap the pieces together, because this is where products are born. Take three ingredients: a smart model (the brain), tools and APIs (the hands and reach), and your own documents via RAG (the private knowledge). Mix them with a clear goal, and you get something people happily pay for.

It's like a kitchen. The chef is the model. The knives, stove, and delivery van are the tools and connectors. The recipe box is your data. None alone is a restaurant — together they serve real meals.

A few real shapes this takes: a **customer-support assistant** that reads your manuals (RAG) and opens tickets (API). A **sales helper** that pulls deal history from your CRM (connector) and drafts follow-ups. A **personal agent** that checks your calendar, books the room, and emails the team — confirming before each big move.

The lesson for you: when you see an impressive AI product, you can now *x-ray* it. Ask, 'What's the brain, what are the hands, and where's its private knowledge?' Almost always, it's these same building blocks, arranged with care.
slides.speakerNotes: Give learners the 'x-ray vision' framing — they should leave able to decompose any product into brain, hands, and knowledge.
slides.marketConnection: Most paid AI products in 2026 — support bots, sales copilots, personal agents — are exactly this recipe of model plus connectors plus private data.

slides.title: Recap: From Clever Talker to Capable Doer
slides.body:
Take a breath — you just learned how AI stops being a genius in a locked room and becomes a teammate with keys, a phone, and a filing cabinet.

Let's gather the threads. **Connections** are what turn answering into doing — letting AI reach the apps you already use, always with your permission. **APIs** are the plugs and sockets that let systems talk in an agreed way, so nobody rebuilds the airline to book a flight. **RAG** is the open-book exam: the AI flips to *your* documents and answers from real facts, not guesses. **Connectors and MCP** are the universal travel adapter — build the plug once, and any AI can use it.

Put them together and you get the recipe behind real products: brain plus hands plus private knowledge. You can now x-ray any AI tool and name its parts.

The big idea to carry forward: intelligence alone is potential; *connection* is what makes it powerful. Mira didn't get smarter on day two — she got connected.

Tomorrow we step out of the engine room and into daily life, to see all of this actually working around you.
slides.speakerNotes: Reinforce the four analogies one last time and end on the 'connection unlocks power' theme to bridge into Day 19.
slides.marketConnection: This brain-plus-hands-plus-knowledge pattern is the blueprint behind nearly every useful AI product shipping in 2026.

keyTakeaways: A smart AI becomes genuinely useful only when it's connected to your apps and data — intelligence is potential, connection is power.
An API is just a 'plug and socket': an agreed way for two systems to talk, so AI can act in the world without rebuilding everything.
RAG is the open-book exam — the AI flips to your own documents and answers from real facts instead of guessing.
Connectors and MCP are a 'universal travel adapter': build the plug once, and any compatible AI can use it instantly.
Real AI products are a simple recipe: a smart model (brain), tools and connectors (hands), and your data via RAG (private knowledge).
Good products ask permission and confirm before big actions — you decide which apps the AI may touch.
marketSnapshot2026: By 2026, 'connection' is the main thing separating a neat chatbot from a product businesses pay for. The MCP standard, which spread rapidly through 2025-2026, means adding a new capability to an AI assistant often feels like installing an app — one-click connectors to Drive, Slack, GitHub, calendars, and CRMs are common. Most enterprise AI deployments now pair a general model with RAG over internal documents so answers reflect the company's own facts, and 'AI agents' that take real actions (booking, ticketing, drafting) with a confirm-before-acting step have moved from demos into everyday tools.
homework: Pick one AI product you've used or heard about (an email helper, a support bot, a coding assistant — anything). In a few sentences, 'x-ray' it: What is the brain (the model)? What are its hands (which apps or APIs does it seem to touch)? Where might its private knowledge come from (your documents, a company database)? You don't need to be right — the goal is to practice seeing the three building blocks behind real products.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow, Day 19, we leave the engine room and walk into ordinary life and work. You'll see all these connected, capable AIs actually showing up — in your inbox, your job, your home — and start spotting them everywhere you look.
